extra.reasoning:
The invariant (opted_out implies not sent) holds on every reachable state. Proof by case analysis on the only transitions that affect sent or opted_out:

- sent becomes true ONLY via t2 (send_attempt), whose guard requires not opted_out, and t2 does not change opted_out. So immediately after t2 the state is opted_out=false, sent=true: antecedent false, property holds.
- opted_out becomes true ONLY via t1 (optout), which atomically sets sent:=false in the same update. So immediately after t1 the state is opted_out=true, sent=false: property holds.
- t3 (send_attempt while opted_out) sets sent:=false and t4 (tick) sets sent:=false; neither can create the bad combination.

For a violation we would need a reachable state with opted_out=true AND sent=true. To have sent=true, the most recent sent-affecting transition must be t2 (the only one setting sent true), which leaves opted_out=false. To then make opted_out=true requires firing t1, but t1 simultaneously resets sent:=false. While opted_out is true, t2's guard (not opted_out) is disabled, so sent can never be re-set to true; t3 and t4 only force sent=false. Hence opted_out=true coexisting with sent=true is unreachable in any execution length (including up to 16 events). The property holds.
